reviews.summary: The paper proposes GTMGC, a Graph Transformer-based model that predicts the ground-state 3D conformation of molecules directly from their 2D graph representation in an end-to-end manner. The core technical contribution is the Molecule Structural Residual Self-Attention (MSRSA) mechanism, which adds learnable adjacency-matrix and row-subtracted distance-matrix bias terms to standard dot-product attention. The model is evaluated on Molecule3D (random and scaffold splits) and QM9, reporting improvements over RDKit (DG/ETKDG), DeeperGCN-DAGNN, GINE, GATv2, and GPS on D-MAE, D-RMSE, and C-RMSD.
strengths: Tackles a useful and well-motivated problem: directly predicting ground-state conformation rather than sampling many candidates.

Reports clear improvements over multiple baselines (RDKit, DeeperGCN-DAGNN, GPS, GATv2, GINE) on both Molecule3D random and scaffold splits, including the C-RMSD metric that captures full conformation alignment.
MSRSA is conceptually simple and adds very few parameters (just γA and γD per head), making it easy to implement and integrate.

Ablations (Tables 2, 3, 4) attempt to isolate the contribution of each component (Laplacian PE, BA, BD, row-subtracted distance), and the row-subtracted distance modification shows a small but consistent benefit.
Authors also show MSRSA transfers to molecular property prediction on QM9, supporting the claim of general applicability.
Code is released, aiding reproducibility.
weaknesses: Limited methodological novelty: using A and D as attention biases is closely related to MAT (Maziarka et al., 2020), Graphormer (Ying et al., 2021), and the Geometric Transformer (Choukroun & Wolf, 2021). The novelty essentially reduces to (i) learnable per-head scalar weights for these biases and (ii) the row-subtracted distance trick, whose justification is somewhat ad hoc and whose effect (~1% C-RMSD improvement) is small.

The decoder uses the predicted Dcache from the encoder, which is itself derived from coordinates produced by an FFN head — but coordinates are not rotationally/translationally canonicalized except via the distance-based loss. The interplay between coordinate prediction and an iterative D-bias is not fully analyzed; there is no comparison to a single-stage variant beyond the ablation removing BD.

Comparison with conformation generation literature (GeoMol, GeoDiff, TorDiff, ConfGF, etc.) is missing. Even acknowledging the task is different (single ground-state vs. ensemble), the authors should compare by selecting the lowest-energy or best-of-N conformer, since these methods are the state of the art for 3D conformation. Without this, the SOTA claim is weak.

The QM9 property-prediction results (Table 6) are substantially worse than equivariant baselines (Equiformer, PaiNN, TorchMD-NET, SphereNet), which undermines the claim that MSRSA is broadly competitive for molecular modeling — the paper presents these as supportive evidence, but they are below average among reported methods.
Statistical rigor is limited: only the ablation reports multiple seeds; main Table 1 numbers have no standard deviations or significance tests.

Several design choices (Laplacian PE specifically vs. alternatives, MoleBERT tokens vs. atom-type IDs) are claimed important, but the ablation in Table 2 shows MoleBERT IDs only marginally beat plain atom IDs on D-MAE/D-RMSE; the C-RMSD gain (~1%) is small and within noise.

Writing has many small grammatical and typographic issues (e.g., 'hole overview', 'creativerow-subtracted', 'The asterisk (*)'). Figures 2 and 4 are hard to parse. Some equations and notations are inconsistent (e.g., dimensions of WQ_l).
The Molecule3D scaffold-split numbers for GINE/GATv2 look surprisingly bad (D-MAE > 1.0), which raises concerns about whether baselines were tuned with comparable effort.
questions: How does GTMGC compare to ensemble-generation methods (GeoMol, GeoDiff, TorDiff) when these are evaluated by selecting the lowest-energy / closest-to-DFT conformer? This comparison seems necessary to support SOTA claims.
Why does the row-subtracted distance matrix help? Can you provide an analysis or theoretical argument beyond the brief 'distance relevance assumption'?
Were the GINE/GATv2/GPS baselines tuned with the same compute and hyperparameter search budget as GTMGC? Their poor performance on scaffold split (Table 1b) suggests possible under-tuning.
Did you experiment with iterating the encoder/decoder distance refinement more than once, similar to recycling in AlphaFold2? Why or why not?
Why does MSRSA underperform equivariant transformers on QM9 property prediction by such a large margin, given that the proposed mechanism is supposed to capture spatial structure well?
Are the main-table results averaged over multiple seeds? What is the variance?
How sensitive is performance to the choice of Laplacian PE dimension and to handling of sign ambiguity in Laplacian eigenvectors?
reviews.limitations: Limitations are not explicitly discussed. The paper does not address (i) potential issues with predicting Cartesian coordinates without explicit equivariance, (ii) failure modes on larger or more flexible molecules outside Molecule3D/QM9, (iii) energetic correctness of predicted conformations (only geometric metrics are reported, not energies), or (iv) the gap to specialized equivariant models on property prediction. No ethical concerns are pertinent.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes REMO, a self-supervised pre-training framework for molecular representation learning that leverages chemical reaction data from USPTO (~1.7M reactions). Two pre-training objectives are introduced: Masked Reaction Centre Reconstruction (MRCR) and Reaction Centre Identification (RCI), where reaction centres in one reactant are masked/identified given other reactants as context. The method is evaluated with GIN and Graphormer encoders on MoleculeACE, ACNet, MoleculeNet, and drug-drug interaction tasks, claiming improvements over standard masked-modeling baselines.
strengths: The motivation is well-articulated: using other reactants as context to disambiguate masked atoms is a sensible inductive bias that constrains the reconstruction problem better than single-molecule masking.

Results on activity-cliff benchmarks (MoleculeACE, ACNet) are notable; the paper claims to be the first deep model to match/exceed ECFP+SVM on these tasks, which has been a known weakness of pre-trained MRL models.
Evaluation covers a reasonable range of downstream tasks: activity cliff, MoleculeNet property prediction, and DDI.
Ablations include continual pre-training on top of AttrMask and GraphMAE, showing complementarity, and there is an entropy analysis showing reaction context reduces reconstruction uncertainty.
weaknesses: Novelty is incremental. Prior work (Wang et al. 2022 'Chemical-reaction-aware molecule representation learning', Broberg et al. 2022, Wen et al. 2022) already uses USPTO reaction data for pre-training. The distinction (focus on reaction centres) is narrow, and direct head-to-head comparisons with these reaction-based pre-training baselines are missing on the same tasks.

Baseline fairness is uneven. On MoleculeNet, REMO uses Graphormer while most baselines use GIN backbones, making it unclear whether gains come from the objective or the encoder. The supervised Graphormer-s row (67.8 avg) is far below all pre-trained methods, suggesting Graphormer specifically benefits from pre-training, but this confound is not isolated.

On MoleculeNet (Table 3), REMO's average (74.7) is only marginally above Mole-BERT (74.3) and the per-task pattern is inconsistent (e.g., ClinTox is notably worse). The claim of state-of-the-art is overstated.
Reaction centre extraction depends on aligned reactant-product graphs and atom mapping; the paper provides little discussion of noise/error in this preprocessing and how it affects pre-training.

The 'proof' in Appendix B is trivial (information gain ≥ 0) and does not actually establish superiority of the learned representations on downstream tasks—only that conditional entropy is lower at pre-training.
Reproducibility details are limited: no code release stated, limited hyperparameter sweeps reported, and 3 seeds is on the low side for the noisy small datasets in MoleculeACE/ACNet.

Presentation has rough edges: typos throughout ('rection', 'predicition', 'biologically' when describing chemistry), figures with inconsistent labeling, and the abstract overstates contributions ('outperforms ... in various downstream tasks' with mixed evidence).

Limitations are not explicitly discussed (e.g., dependence on availability of reaction data, bias toward USPTO chemistry, applicability to molecules whose reactivity is poorly represented in patents).
questions: How does REMO compare directly against Wang et al. (2022) MolR and Broberg et al. (2022) on the same downstream benchmarks with the same encoder? These are the most relevant baselines and seem missing.
Can you isolate the encoder effect on MoleculeNet by reporting Graphormer with standard AttrMask/GraphMAE pre-training, and GIN-based REMO, on the full benchmark?
How sensitive is REMO to errors in reaction centre detection? What fraction of USPTO reactions have unambiguous centres, and what happens if centres are noisy or multiple plausible centres exist?
Why does REMO underperform on ClinTox (64.6 vs 82.3 for GraphMAE)? Is this related to the chemistry coverage of USPTO?
For the activity cliff results, can you report variance across seeds for REMO on MoleculeACE (Table 1 reports only point estimates)?
How is the 2401-token vocabulary for reaction centre reconstruction constructed, and how does class imbalance in this vocabulary affect optimization?
reviews.limitations: Limitations are not adequately addressed. The paper does not discuss reliance on reaction-mapped USPTO data, possible chemistry distribution bias, sensitivity to reaction centre extraction errors, or scenarios where reaction context is unavailable. No ethical concerns identified beyond standard drug-discovery dual-use considerations, which are not discussed.

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of reconstructing a hypergraph from its clique-expansion projection. It provides theoretical analysis identifying two structural patterns (violating Sperner/conformal properties) that cause information loss, and shows worst-case combinatorial impossibility. It then proposes a learning-based framework (SHyRe) that uses a separate hypergraph from the same domain as training data, consisting of (1) a clique sampler optimized via a submodular greedy procedure leveraging an empirical statistic rho(n,k) that is shown to be domain-invariant, and (2) a hyperedge classifier using count-based or motif-based features. Experiments on 8 datasets demonstrate strong improvements over baselines, plus use cases in protein essentiality ranking, link prediction, and node clustering.
strengths: Well-motivated problem framing: the paper clearly articulates a previously under-explored question (hypergraph reconstruction from projection) and grounds it in real applications (PPI networks, coauthorship, social sensors).

Solid theoretical contribution: Theorems 1-3 give a clean characterization of when reconstruction is hard, identifying Error I/II patterns tied to Sperner and conformality, and provide a worst-case lower bound on max-clique reconstruction accuracy.
The rho(n,k) statistic and its empirical 'alignment' across train/query splits is an interesting and well-visualized observation that motivates the cross-domain learning setting.
The clique sampler with submodular-style optimization and a (1-1/e) approximation guarantee (Theorem 4) is a principled algorithmic contribution.

Comprehensive empirical evaluation: 8 datasets, 7+ baselines, semi-supervised and transfer learning settings, ablations on the sampler, multiple downstream tasks (protein ranking, link prediction, clustering), running time analysis, and extensions to edge multiplicities and node-degree-preserving projections.
Reproducibility appears reasonable (code link provided, hyperparameters described).
weaknesses: The supervised setting assumes access to a training hypergraph H0 from the same distribution; while motivated, this assumption is strong and the paper could more clearly delineate when such an H0 is realistically available versus when one is stuck with only G1. The transfer learning experiment (Fig 6) is helpful but limited to coauthorship.

Some baselines (especially hyperedge prediction methods like Hyper-SAGNN, CMM) are adapted in ways that significantly disadvantage them (Appx C.3 acknowledges this). The very poor numbers (<1% Jaccard) suggest the comparison may not be fully fair, though the authors do explain incompatibility.

The feature extractors (count, motif) are somewhat heuristic and hand-engineered; there is no comparison with stronger learned representations (e.g., subgraph GNNs, structure-aware models) that could be applied to candidate cliques.

The complexity analysis relies on the empirical observation that |M| ~ |E| in practice; while Appx D.1 discusses k-degeneracy and c-closure justifications, the worst-case scalability remains a concern and is not stress-tested on truly large/dense graphs.

The downstream task results (link prediction in Table 9) show only modest improvements over the projected graph (often <2 AUC points), which weakens the claim that reconstructed hypergraphs are broadly useful as intermediate representations.
The protein use case (Table 8) shows a qualitative top-10 list shift but the quantitative evidence (Pearson 0.93 vs 0.88) is modest and based on a single dataset pair.
Numerical stability analysis of rho(n,k) (Appx D.2) is simulation-based and limited; no theoretical guarantee on robustness to distribution shift.
Presentation: the paper is dense and some notation (e.g., the En,k / Qn,k indexing, the Algorithm 1 subroutine) requires careful reading; Figure 4 heatmaps are hard to read at the printed size.
questions: How sensitive is the method to the size and quality of H0? The 20% semi-supervised result is encouraging, but what about 5% or 1%? At what point does performance degrade meaningfully?
For the transfer learning setting, can you provide results on more diverse domain shifts (e.g., training on social interactions, testing on coauthorship)?
Could you compare against a learned subgraph/clique GNN baseline as a hyperedge classifier feature extractor, to isolate the contribution of count/motif features vs. the overall framework?
How does performance scale with graph size? Datasets used are relatively small (largest |E|~200K). Can the method handle million-scale graphs where |M| may explode?
In the clique sampler, how is beta chosen in practice? The paper says it is tuned on a validation split but the sensitivity of final performance to beta would be informative.
For Theorem 4, can you provide empirical evidence on how tight the (1-1/e) bound is in practice, i.e., how close q is to q*?
Why does SHyRe-count sometimes outperform SHyRe-motif despite motif features being more 'principled'? Are there dataset characteristics that predict which is better?
reviews.limitations: The authors discuss broader impacts in Appx E, including concerns that smaller hyperedges (e.g., papers with fewer authors) are harder to reconstruct, with implications for marginalized groups. They propose mitigations. Future work directions (end-to-end optimization, attribute use) are discussed in Appx G. Limitations regarding worst-case complexity, distribution-shift assumptions, and the need for H0 are partially but not fully acknowledged. No major ethical concerns beyond the broader-impact note.

final.summary: The paper studies the problem of reconstructing a hypergraph from its clique-expansion projection. It provides theoretical analysis identifying two structural patterns (violating Sperner/conformal properties) that cause information loss, and shows worst-case combinatorial impossibility. It then proposes a learning-based framework (SHyRe) that uses a separate hypergraph from the same domain as training data, consisting of (1) a clique sampler optimized via a submodular greedy procedure leveraging an empirical statistic rho(n,k) that is shown to be domain-invariant, and (2) a hyperedge classifier using count-based or motif-based features. Experiments on 8 datasets demonstrate strong improvements over baselines, plus use cases in protein essentiality ranking, link prediction, and node clustering.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes 'visual evidence prompting,' a method that uses outputs from small specialized visual models (object detectors and scene graph generators) as textual context prompts to mitigate hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). The authors also introduce a new benchmark for relation hallucination based on Visual Genome. Experiments on mPLUG-Owl, MiniGPT-4, and Qwen-VL-Chat show improvements over baselines and the LRV-Instruction tuning approach on POPE-style object hallucination evaluation and the proposed relation benchmark.
strengths: The idea of complementing LVLMs with specialized small visual models is intuitive and practical; it avoids costly fine-tuning and provides interpretable, modular evidence.
Introduction of a relation hallucination benchmark fills a gap, as most prior work (e.g., POPE) focuses only on object existence.
Reasonably comprehensive empirical evaluation across three LVLMs and multiple detection/SGG models, including analyses of erroneous evidence, prompt templates, and out-of-domain images.
The method consistently improves over baselines on the binary yes/no hallucination evaluation, and the analysis showing robustness to incorrect evidence is informative.
weaknesses: Limited technical novelty: the method amounts to concatenating detector/SGG outputs into the prompt. This is a well-known idea in the LLM tool-use / agent / 'verbalized perception' literature (e.g., MM-ReAct, Visual ChatGPT, Chameleon, LENS), and the paper does not adequately compare to or even cite such prior work.

Evaluation is largely restricted to constrained yes/no POPE-style probes. Hallucinations in real generation (open-ended captions, the very type shown in Fig. 1) are not quantitatively evaluated using metrics like CHAIR, GPT-4 evaluation, or human studies. This is a significant gap given the paper's framing.

Comparison to baselines is unfair in some ways: LRV-Instruction is one specific tuning method; stronger contemporary baselines such as Woodpecker, LURE, or other tool-augmented LVLMs are not compared. Also, the method essentially leaks ground-truth-like object/relation information from supervised detectors trained on related datasets (COCO, VG), which raises questions about whether improvements reflect a real method advance or label leakage.

The proposed relation hallucination benchmark is constructed from VG and uses an SGG model trained on VG, which creates train/test domain alignment that may inflate gains. Although authors state no overlap with VG splits, the visual evidence model still benefits from VG supervision.

Writing has numerous grammatical errors and awkward phrasings (e.g., 'this work explores how the hallucinations... can be mitigates naturally', 'mitigates naturally in LVLMs'), and some claims are overstated ('first to study visual evidence', 'minimal strongest baseline').

Tables and figures are poorly formatted; multiple appendix references appear inconsistently (e.g., 'Table 16' referenced but labelled as Table 1), and the paper contains revision artifacts (e.g., 'List of Revisions' with reviewer IDs in the submission), suggesting this is a revised version uploaded for review rather than a clean submission.
The robustness-to-erroneous-evidence analysis is shallow: the false-evidence rate is only ~6.9%, and the analysis does not stress-test the method with adversarially incorrect evidence.
No discussion of inference cost overhead of running both a detector/SGG model and the LVLM, nor of latency/practical deployment considerations.
questions: How does the method compare to existing tool-augmented or verbalized-perception approaches such as MM-ReAct, Chameleon, LENS, or Woodpecker, which use similar ideas of feeding detector outputs into LLMs/LVLMs?
Can you provide evaluations on open-ended generation hallucination metrics (CHAIR, GPT-4-judge, human eval) rather than only POPE-style yes/no probes?

Since the SGG model used for evidence is trained on Visual Genome and the relation benchmark is also constructed from VG, how do you rule out that gains stem from label leakage rather than a generalizable improvement?
What is the inference overhead of incorporating the small visual model(s)? How does the combined system compare in cost/performance to simply using a larger LVLM?
How does the method handle attributes, counting, or fine-grained hallucinations that go beyond object existence and binary relations?
How sensitive is the method to the choice of evidence formatting? Beyond the prompt template ablation, did you explore structured vs. natural language evidence?
reviews.limitations: The paper briefly mentions limitations of relying on visual model quality and acknowledges that erroneous evidence can mislead the LVLM, but it does not seriously discuss (a) computational overhead, (b) limitations to closed-vocabulary detectors/SGG models, (c) the narrow scope of the evaluation (yes/no probes), or (d) potential train-test leakage between the SGG model's training data and the relation benchmark. No ethical concerns are discussed, although they are limited here.

reviews.summary: The paper argues that standard diffusion models (e.g., DDPM) suffer from an 'expressive bottleneck' because the backward conditional p_theta(x_{t-1}|x_t) is parameterized as a single Gaussian while the true posterior q(x_{t-1}|x_t) for multimodal data (e.g., Gaussian mixtures) is itself a mixture. The authors prove that for Gaussian mixture data the local and global denoising errors of standard DMs can be made arbitrarily large, undermining 'bounded score error' assumptions in prior convergence proofs. They propose Soft Mixture Denoising (SMD), which introduces a latent variable z_t (generated by a small hypernetwork from noise) that modulates the parameters of a Gaussian backward kernel, yielding a continuous mixture. They derive an upper-bound loss similar to DDPM's, and show empirical improvements in FID across CIFAR-10, LSUN, and CelebA-HQ, especially at small T.
strengths: The observation that q(x_{t-1}|x_t) is a mixture when q(x_0) is a mixture is correct and presented clearly, with a clean derivation in the appendix (Eq. 29).

The proposed SMD is simple and lightweight: it adds a latent z_t produced by a small network, modulating the denoiser via a hypernetwork-style mechanism, and the training loss closely mirrors the standard DDPM loss, making it easy to integrate.

Experiments span several diffusion backbones (DDPM, ADM, LDM, DDIM) and several datasets/resolutions, and report consistent (if modest) FID improvements, with particularly notable gains at low sampling step counts (Fig. 3).
The toy 7x7 Gaussian experiment (Fig. 2) gives a nice qualitative illustration of the claimed bottleneck.
weaknesses: The 'expressive bottleneck' argument is well-known in the diffusion literature. Mixture/multimodal backward kernels and the fact that the Gaussian-posterior approximation only holds in the small-step limit have been discussed by Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015) and explored in works on learnable variance (Nichol & Dhariwal Improved DDPM), analytic-DPM, and various non-Gaussian / mixture / energy-based reverse processes. The paper does not adequately cite or compare against this body of work, overstating novelty.

Theorems 3.1 and 3.2 essentially say 'a single Gaussian cannot approximate an arbitrary mixture'—which is true but rather trivial and only states existence of a bad q(x_0). They do not quantify how the error scales with T, the noise schedule, or properties of the data, so the claim that they 'undermine' Chen et al. 2023 / Lee et al. 2022 is overstated: those works assume bounded score error per-step, which is a learnability assumption, not a representability one, and the bottleneck shrinks as T grows (as the authors themselves note).

The methodological novelty of SMD is limited: it amounts to feeding an extra latent noise z_t into the denoiser via a hypernetwork. This is essentially a stochastic/noise-conditioned denoiser and is closely related to existing ideas (e.g., latent-conditioned diffusion, denoising auto-encoders with additional noise inputs, hierarchical/auxiliary-variable diffusion). The paper does not compare to learned-variance DDPM, mixture-output DDPM variants, or other expressive backward kernels.

Theorem 4.1 (zero error under SMD) relies on the same kind of global-optimum assumption that the authors criticize others for making. The construction in Appendix E uses arbitrary partitions of latent space and step-function hypernetworks, which says little about what an actually trained SMD will achieve. So the theoretical 'fix' is not stronger than the prior guarantees it critiques.

Empirical gains, while consistent, are modest (FID drops of ~0.3–0.7 in most cases at T=1000) and there is no reporting of variance over seeds, no statistical significance, no comparison with strong fast-sampler baselines (DPM-Solver, EDM, progressive distillation) for the low-T claim, and no comparison to learned-variance or mixture-output baselines that target the same problem.

Reproducibility details are thin: hyperparameters, training compute, architecture of f_phi and g_xi, exact T schedules, and the LDM setup are largely absent from the main text and appendix. The added parameter count of the hypernetwork is not reported, making the comparison potentially unfair.

Presentation has notable issues: many typos (e.g., 'espeically'), inconsistent notation (\bar\theta vs \theta_k), Fig. 3 caption says 'F1 scores' for image generation (should be FID), and the discussion of why diffusion works well 'in practice' is hand-wavy.

The paper does not engage with continuous-time / SDE views where the score is the central object and the 'Gaussian reverse' is exact in the infinitesimal limit, which weakens the framing of the bottleneck as a fundamental flaw.
questions: How does SMD compare to learned-variance DDPM (Nichol & Dhariwal) and to explicit mixture-of-Gaussians reverse kernels? These are direct baselines for addressing the same bottleneck.
What is the additional parameter count and wall-clock cost (training and sampling) introduced by f_phi and g_xi? Are FID gains preserved when the baseline is given equivalent extra capacity?
Could you report FID with multiple seeds and confidence intervals, particularly for the low-T regime where the claimed advantage is largest?
For the low-T comparison in Fig. 3, how does SMD compare to dedicated fast samplers such as DPM-Solver, EDM, or distillation methods, which are the state of the art for few-step sampling?

Theorem 3.1 establishes existence of a bad q(x_0). Can you quantify how M_t depends on T and the noise schedule, to clarify the practical severity of the bottleneck rather than only an existence result?

Theorem 4.1 assumes global optimization. Can you provide any empirical or theoretical evidence that the trained SMD actually approaches a mixture posterior (e.g., on the toy GMM, measure KL between learned p_theta(x_{t-1}|x_t) and the true mixture)?
Is the latent z_t at sampling time drawn independently per step, and how sensitive are results to its dimensionality and to the architecture of g_xi?
reviews.limitations: Limitations are barely discussed. The authors do not acknowledge that (i) their existence-type theorems do not quantify the practical severity of the bottleneck, (ii) their SMD guarantee assumes global optimization just like the works they critique, (iii) they do not compare to existing methods that target the same issue (learned variance, mixture outputs), and (iv) the extra computational cost / parameter count of SMD is not analyzed. No ethical or societal-impact concerns beyond standard generative-modeling issues, and none are discussed.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes FMN (Forget-Me-Not), a training mechanism for injecting backdoors that are robust to fine-tuning-based defenses. FMN combines two ideas: (1) training the poisoned model with a cyclical learning rate schedule (inspired by super-finetuning), and (2) interleaving clean-only training epochs with poisoned training epochs to simulate finetuning during backdoor injection. Experiments on CIFAR-10, CelebA, and ImageNet-10 with several backdoor attacks (Blend, Lp inv, Trojaning, Input-aware, LIRA, Narcissus) show that FMN-trained backdoors survive standard FT, super-FT, FT-SAM, and NAD better than conventional backdoor training.
strengths: The problem of building finetuning-resistant backdoors is well-motivated and practically relevant, especially given recent work (super-finetuning, FT-SAM) that effectively erases standard backdoors.
The proposed mechanism is simple, intuitive, and can be plugged into many existing backdoor attacks without redesigning trigger functions.

Experiments span multiple datasets (CIFAR-10, CelebA, ImageNet-10), multiple attack types (patch-based, blended, optimized, clean-label), and several finetuning-based defenses; results consistently show meaningful improvements in post-finetuning ASR.
Ablations separate the contributions of cyclical learning rate and clean-backdoor interleaving, and a transfer-learning experiment explores backdoor persistence under task change.
The paper also checks that FMN does not break stealth properties of LIRA against non-finetuning defenses (Neural Cleanse, Fine-Pruning, ANP, STRIP).
weaknesses: Novelty is somewhat limited: the method is essentially 'apply super-finetuning-style cyclical LR during backdoor training' plus alternating clean/poisoned epochs. There is no theoretical analysis to back the loss-landscape intuition shown in Figure 1, which remains a cartoon.

Experimental rigor concerns: results are reported as single numbers without standard deviations or multiple seeds, and no statistical significance is provided. Given the cyclical LR's sensitivity, variance could be substantial.

BadNets is dropped because it 'fails to converge' under FMN, which is a notable limitation but treated dismissively; this suggests the method may not generalize to simpler/strong triggers and the convergence behavior is not analyzed.

Defense coverage is incomplete. Important recent finetuning-related defenses (e.g., I-BAU, RNP (Li et al., 2023, cited but not evaluated), ABL, MCR/mode-connectivity, weight averaging, larger finetuning data fractions) are not evaluated. The defender's hyperparameters (e.g., super-FT epochs, LR schedule) are taken as fixed; an adaptive defender aware of FMN is never considered, which is the standard adaptive-attack/adaptive-defense convention in this literature.

FT-SAM is reimplemented by the authors because code was unavailable; details are sparse and ρ=2 is large. Without comparison to the original results or sensitivity studies, the FT-SAM numbers are hard to trust.

The transfer-learning experiment (Table 3) is small (only Blend and Trojaning, only ImageNet-10→ImageNet-10-FT) and the 'attack success' criterion (accuracy drop on backdoor inputs in a new label space) is not well-defined: the target class no longer exists, so the metric measures generic disruption, not a controlled targeted attack.

Presentation issues: Figure 1's right panel is not quantitatively grounded; several claims ('hybrid loss landscape', 'deep-rooted backdoor') are hand-wavy; the threshold of 50% ASR for 'attack success' is arbitrary and not justified.

No discussion or evaluation of why interleaving clean training does not simply erase the backdoor during training itself — convergence dynamics, poisoning ratio sensitivity, and effect of the number of clean epochs are not studied.
Reproducibility: no code is promised, several hyperparameters are under-specified (e.g., number of cyclical periods, n iterations in each ramp, exact schedule for ImageNet-10/CelebA).
questions: Can you provide multi-seed results with standard deviations for Tables 1, 2, and 4? How stable is FMN to the choice of LR_MAX1, LR_MAX2, and the number of cycles?

Why does BadNets fail to converge under FMN, and does this indicate FMN only works when triggers are sufficiently 'entangled' with image content? Have you tried mitigations (e.g., higher poison rate, smaller clean-epoch fraction)?
How does FMN fare against an adaptive defender that knows FMN is being used — for example, super-FT with longer schedules, larger clean data fractions, or RNP (Li et al., 2023) and I-BAU?

In the transfer learning setting (Table 3), how is ASR/accuracy defined when the target label is not in the downstream label space? Could the observed drop simply reflect distribution shift rather than backdoor persistence?
Did you verify your FT-SAM reimplementation reproduces the numbers from Zhu et al. (2023) on standard backdoors before evaluating it against FMN?
What is the sensitivity to the poison ratio (you use 5% throughout)? Does FMN still work at 1% or in clean-label-only regimes?
Have you analyzed the loss landscape empirically (e.g., via 1D/2D loss surface plots, sharpness measures) to support the qualitative claim in Figure 1?
reviews.limitations: The authors acknowledge that FMN requires full control of training (white-box attacker) and suggest black-box extensions as future work. They do not adequately discuss: (i) failure on BadNets, (ii) lack of multi-seed/statistical analysis, (iii) absence of adaptive defenses, (iv) ambiguity of the transfer-learning metric. Ethical/societal implications are touched on only briefly ('use trusted providers'), with no concrete discussion of dual-use risk or responsible disclosure.

final.summary: The paper proposes FMN (Forget-Me-Not), a training mechanism for injecting backdoors that are robust to fine-tuning-based defenses. FMN combines two ideas: (1) training the poisoned model with a cyclical learning rate schedule (inspired by super-finetuning), and (2) interleaving clean-only training epochs with poisoned training epochs to simulate finetuning during backdoor injection. Experiments on CIFAR-10, CelebA, and ImageNet-10 with several backdoor attacks (Blend, Lp inv, Trojaning, Input-aware, LIRA, Narcissus) show that FMN-trained backdoors survive standard FT, super-FT, FT-SAM, and NAD better than conventional backdoor training.
strengths: The problem of building finetuning-resistant backdoors is well-motivated and practically relevant, especially given recent work (super-finetuning, FT-SAM) that effectively erases standard backdoors.
The proposed mechanism is simple, intuitive, and can be plugged into many existing backdoor attacks without redesigning trigger functions.

Experiments span multiple datasets (CIFAR-10, CelebA, ImageNet-10), multiple attack types (patch-based, blended, optimized, clean-label), and several finetuning-based defenses; results consistently show meaningful improvements in post-finetuning ASR.
Ablations separate the contributions of cyclical learning rate and clean-backdoor interleaving, and a transfer-learning experiment explores backdoor persistence under task change.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes CASPR (Combining Axes Preconditioners), an adaptive preconditioning method for deep learning that combines per-axis preconditioners using a Kronecker-sum structure instead of the Kronecker-product structure used in Shampoo. The authors prove that CASPR's preconditioner is sandwiched between Shampoo and full-matrix Adagrad in Loewner order, derive a tighter regret bound in the OCO framework, and demonstrate improvements over Shampoo and AdamW on GNN (OGBG-molpcba), Transformer (universal dependencies), and autoregressive language modeling (C4).
strengths: The key idea — replacing Shampoo's Kronecker product (geometric mean of axis preconditioners) with an arithmetic+geometric mean combination via Kronecker sum — is simple, elegant, and well-motivated by the Loewner ordering argument.

Theoretical analysis is clean: Lemma 3.2 shows Shampoo is a limiting case of a CASPR family, Lemma 3.3 establishes the Loewner sandwich, and Theorem 4.1 gives a strictly tighter regret bound than Shampoo with the same asymptotic O(√T) rate.
The implementation is a minimal modification of Shampoo (one extra preconditioning step) and adds essentially no wall-clock overhead, making practical adoption straightforward.
Empirical evaluation spans multiple architectures (GNN, encoder Transformer, decoder LLM) and includes a 234M-parameter language model on C4, which is non-trivial scale.
The approximation-error experiment (Figure 1) provides useful empirical evidence that CASPR is closer to full-matrix Adagrad than Shampoo or single-axis preconditioners.
Standard deviations are reported across seeds (Table 5), and hyperparameter search ranges are documented.
weaknesses: Empirical gains are modest: 1.4% relative test AP improvement over Shampoo on OGBG-molpcba, 1% relative validation error improvement on UD, and ~1% log perplexity on C4. While consistent, the gains are small and it is unclear whether they justify the additional algorithmic complexity for practitioners already using Shampoo.
The C4 LM experiments report only single runs (no error bars in Figure 4), and Adam is omitted from these comparisons due to cost, weakening the strongest scale claim in the paper.

The theoretical advantage (regret bound) holds only up to constants in the OCO setting; the gap between CASPR's tr((L^{-1/4}⊗I + I⊗R^{-1/4})/2)^{-2} and Shampoo's tr(L^{1/4}⊗R^{1/4}) is not quantified meaningfully to suggest when it would translate to large empirical gains.

Missing comparison to other recent Kronecker-factored approaches such as K-FAC, Tensor Normal Training (Ren & Goldfarb 2021), or Kronecker-factored quasi-Newton methods, which are mentioned in related work but not benchmarked.

The approximation experiment in Figure 1 is conducted on downsampled 128x128 gradients, not the actual parameter sizes used during training, raising questions about how representative the approximation quality is at scale.

Hyperparameter search budgets differ across methods in some experiments (e.g., AdamW gets 3.53x more iterations to match wall-clock), which is reasonable but the search spaces and tuning effort relative to compute budget should be discussed more carefully.

The claim that CASPR 'is nearer to full-matrix Adagrad' is shown only in Loewner order on one side; the upper bound √r(rεI + Σg g^T)^{-1/2} ⪰ CASPR involves a scale factor √r that is absorbed into η, making 'nearness' somewhat informal.
The 234M parameter LLM is run for only 42B tokens, which is well below typical compute-optimal scaling; whether the gap persists or shrinks at larger scale is unaddressed.
questions: How does CASPR compare to K-FAC and Tensor Normal Training empirically? These are the most natural Kronecker-factored baselines and are referenced but not evaluated.
Do the LM experiments in Figure 4 have any variance estimate? Single-seed LM training curves are difficult to interpret given the small gap.
What happens at larger scales (e.g., billion-parameter LLMs with more tokens)? Does the gap to Shampoo grow, persist, or shrink?
Can you quantify, theoretically or empirically, how much of the regret bound improvement over Shampoo translates to convergence speed in practice?
For multidimensional parameters (Appendix A.9), how sensitive are results to the choice of dimension merging? Have you tried treating each tensor axis with its own preconditioner instead of merging?
In the approximation experiment, why downsample to 128x128? Have you verified that the qualitative ordering of methods holds at full size on a smaller layer?

The CASPR update applies the preconditioning twice sequentially (Ut := L^{-1/4} Ut + Ut R^{-1/4} applied twice). Is this exactly equivalent to applying the matrix ((L^{-1/4}⊗I + I⊗R^{-1/4})/2)^2 to vec(G), and what is the numerical stability compared to Shampoo?
reviews.limitations: The authors briefly discuss societal impact (Appendix A.5) and acknowledge that adapting CASPR to Fisher-based methods is future work. However, they do not adequately discuss limitations such as: (i) the modest size of empirical gains, (ii) the assumption that L and R commute in some of the Loewner-order arguments, (iii) the impact of the blocking strategy used to handle very large layers, and (iv) potential numerical issues with the double preconditioning step. No ethical concerns.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Split-N-Denoise (SnD), a framework for privacy-preserving LLM inference under local differential privacy (dχ-privacy). The token embedding layer runs on the client side, the client adds Laplacian noise to embeddings before sending them to the server, the server runs the rest of the LLM and returns the (noisy) output embedding, and finally the client applies a pre-trained transformer denoiser that conditions on the noisy output, the raw token embeddings, and the specific noise vectors. The authors evaluate on BERT, GPT-2, and T5 variants across several GLUE-style tasks and report >10% average improvement over TokEmbPriv, Text2Text, and RAPT baselines, plus inference attack evaluations.
strengths: Addresses a timely and practical problem (privacy in EaaS / LLM inference) with a clean architectural idea: move denoising to the client where the actual noise realizations are known.
The framework is evaluated across three LLM families (BERT, GPT-2, T5) and multiple model sizes, and compared against three relevant baselines (TokEmbPriv, Text2Text, RAPT).
Includes both empirical privacy evaluation (embedding inversion and attribute inference attacks) and utility evaluation, plus ablations on norm clipping and server-side vs client-side denoising.
Overhead analysis shows substantial efficiency advantages over cryptographic alternatives (PCFT, Iron) and quantifies client compute savings vs full local inference.
weaknesses: The privacy claim is questionable in practice. The client must send the denoiser the raw token embeddings X̃ alongside the noisy output (Eq. 5–6). The denoiser is trained on the server but runs on the client; nevertheless, the framework's privacy argument only covers what is sent to the server. The interaction between (a) the privacy budget η applied to embeddings, (b) the fact that the denoiser was trained by the server to invert noise, and (c) whether attribute/inversion attacks were performed against the *post-denoise* embedding (which is what an EaaS client would actually use downstream) is not clearly addressed.

The dχ-privacy guarantee uses unusually large η values (e.g., η=100, 500 for BERT, η=1 to 100 for GPT-2). Under dχ-privacy with L2 distance over token embeddings, such η values provide extremely weak formal protection; the paper does not translate these into interpretable (ε,δ)-DP statements or compare meaningfully to standard DP literature. Sequence-level composition across n tokens is also not discussed.

Utility numbers in Tables 1–3 are very close to the η=∞ baseline (a few percent gap), but the η=∞ accuracies themselves are quite low (e.g., RTE ~0.6, QQP ~0.7 with no fine-tuning), suggesting the embeddings + simple MLP classifier setup is weak. It is unclear whether SnD's gains over baselines persist in a stronger evaluation pipeline.

The denoiser is trained per noise-level group and per model, requiring substantial server-side effort and a separate model per LLM/η partition (Appendix A.6.3). This complicates deployment and the privacy story (the server tailors a denoiser to each η, which is itself information about the client's privacy setting).

Baselines (TokEmbPriv, Text2Text) are essentially noise-only methods with no learned post-processing; the comparison would be much fairer if those baselines were also given a client-side or server-side learned reconstruction module trained on the same public data.

Proposition 1's lower bound on server-side denoising MSE is used to motivate client-side denoising, but the bound is loose (depends on Bx and unbiasedness) and does not establish that client-side denoising must be better—only that this particular server-side bound is large. No matching bound or analysis is provided for the client-side denoiser.

The denoise model architecture and training details are somewhat sparse; reproducibility relies on appendix tables but no code is mentioned, and the choice to train one denoiser per η-group is presented without ablation on its impact.

Authors acknowledge in Appendix A.12 that the method does not yet scale to larger models (LLaMA, OPT-6.7B) for downstream accuracy, which undermines the headline framing about 'large language models'.

Presentation has multiple typos and rough sentences (e.g., 'an significant accuracy improvement', 'privacy protecti throughout', 'unconver the closed-form mapping'); Figure 1 is described but not very informative.
questions: Can the authors translate the chosen η values into standard (ε,δ)-DP guarantees, and discuss sequence-level composition across n tokens? At η=100 or 500 with L2 distance, what is the effective privacy?

Were the embedding inversion and attribute inference attacks performed on the privatized token embeddings sent to the server, or on the post-denoise embeddings the client uses for downstream tasks? Since the denoiser is trained to undo the noise, does it leak information when attacked?
How is the denoiser actually deployed under varying η at inference time? Must the client download a different denoiser per η-bucket, and does specifying η to the server reveal information?
If the baselines (TokEmbPriv, Text2Text) were augmented with a comparable learned reconstruction head trained on the same public corpus, how would the comparison change?
Why are absolute downstream accuracies so low at η=∞ (e.g., RTE ~0.6)? Would fine-tuning the classifier head more carefully, or using stronger pooling, change the relative gains of SnD?
Can the authors clarify the threat model more precisely: what exactly does the server see, what does a curious client see, and what is the trust assumption regarding the denoiser the server provides?
How sensitive is performance to the size of the public corpus used to train the denoiser, and to distribution mismatch between that corpus and the downstream task?
reviews.limitations: The authors briefly discuss scalability to larger LLMs, user compute cost of the denoiser, and extension to seq2seq inference in Appendix A.12, which is appreciated. However, important limitations are not adequately addressed: (i) the formal privacy meaning of the chosen η values and how they compose over a sequence, (ii) whether the post-denoise embedding (which the client uses downstream) still satisfies the claimed privacy guarantee against an adversary who has the denoiser, (iii) the need to train and distribute multiple denoiser models per η-bucket per LLM, and (iv) the gap between strong relative gains and weak absolute downstream accuracies. No ethical concerns beyond standard privacy considerations.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes LipVoicer, a lip-to-speech synthesis method that combines a video-conditioned diffusion model (MelGen) generating mel-spectrograms with classifier guidance from a pre-trained ASR using text predicted by a lip-reading network. A DiffWave vocoder converts the mel-spectrogram to audio. Experiments on LRS2 and LRS3 (and GRID in appendix) show substantial WER and MOS improvements over baselines (VCA-GAN, Lip2Speech, SVTS).
strengths: Sensible and well-motivated design: leveraging a strong lip-reader plus ASR classifier guidance at inference is a clean way to inject text information without complex alignment, and the modularity (swap the lip-reader/ASR without retraining) is appealing.

Strong empirical results on challenging in-the-wild datasets (LRS2, LRS3), with large WER reductions (e.g., 21.4% vs 57-90% for baselines on LRS3) and consistent MOS gains across naturalness, intelligibility, quality, and synchronization.

Comprehensive evaluation: human MOS with many annotators per sample and careful filtering, plus multiple objective metrics (non-intrusive STOI-Net, DNSMOS, SyncNet LSE-C/D, WER), and a demo page allowing qualitative verification.
Useful ablations on guidance weights ω1, ω2, lip-reader accuracy, ASR quality, vocoder choice, face embedding, and training data scale, including comparison with ground-truth text as an upper bound.
Thoughtful discussion of why intrusive metrics (PESQ/STOI) are inappropriate for this generative task, supported by a voice-conversion sanity-check experiment.
weaknesses: Methodological novelty is moderate: classifier guidance with ASR has been previously used in TTS (Guided-TTS, Kim et al. 2022), and the contribution is largely the application to lip-to-speech with a pre-trained lip-reader. The novelty is more in combination than in individual components.

Concurrent diffusion-based and unit-based methods (DiffV2S, Choi et al. 2023b, ReVISE) are only partially compared. ReVISE comparison is indirect (only via a single ASR's WER number on different test sets) since the authors could not run it; this leaves the comparison to the closest contemporary baselines incomplete.

The choice of ASR used for WER evaluation (Ma et al., 2023) is the same family as the lip-reader, and the guiding ASR shares architecture with Burchi & Timofte (2023). There is a possible bias when the evaluation ASR is similar to the one used to guide generation; an additional, independent ASR for WER evaluation would strengthen claims.

Hyperparameter sensitivity is significant (different ω1, ω2, tASR per dataset, selected via listening tests/grid search); reproducibility of these choices and how they were tuned (especially using listening tests) is not fully transparent and risks tuning on test set.

Inference cost is not reported. Diffusion sampling with classifier guidance (requiring ASR gradients at each step) plus DiffWave vocoder is likely expensive; a runtime/efficiency comparison vs baselines would be valuable.

The method inherits errors from the lip reader. While the ablation shows graceful degradation, in realistic scenarios with poor lighting/angles lip-reading WER may be much higher, and the paper doesn't probe robustness in such conditions.

Limited analysis of speaker identity preservation. The face image is used as global conditioning, but there is no quantitative speaker similarity evaluation (e.g., speaker embedding cosine similarity) to assess how well speaker characteristics are preserved.
questions: How sensitive are results to the test-set tuning of ω1, ω2, tASR? Were these chosen on a held-out validation split or on the test set?
Can you report WER using a third, independent ASR (not from Ma et al. or Burchi & Timofte families) to rule out evaluation bias?
What is the inference time / compute cost per second of generated audio compared to SVTS, VCA-GAN, Lip2Speech, and ReVISE?
How does LipVoicer perform when the lip-reader degrades significantly (e.g., profile views, occlusions)? Does the ASR guidance amplify errors when the predicted text is wrong?
How well is speaker identity preserved? Could you report a speaker similarity metric between generated and ground-truth audio?
Have you compared directly (even on a small subset) to DiffV2S (Choi et al. 2023a) and Choi et al. (2023b), which are the most relevant concurrent works?
Why does setting w1 = -1 (only unconditional video noise) still yield 30.1% WER? This seems surprisingly low given the unconditional model — please clarify.
reviews.limitations: The authors acknowledge potential misuse for misrepresentation/manipulation, which is appropriate but brief. Technical limitations (dependence on lip-reader quality, inference cost, hyperparameter sensitivity, speaker identity preservation) are not explicitly discussed. No major ethical concerns beyond standard generative-audio dual-use issues; using LRS2/LRS3 (public datasets of real speakers) is standard but raises mild consent concerns that are not discussed.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes PEEP, a metric learning framework for enzyme function (EC number) prediction. It builds on the MoCo-style contrastive learning pipeline of CLEAN and adds three biology-motivated components: (1) fusing SMILES embeddings of cognate ligands into protein sequence representations, (2) a self-attention module over residues (with a sparse variant) intended to highlight active-site residues, and (3) an EC-hierarchy-aware regularization term that scales similarity penalties by the depth of EC-number disagreement. Experiments on Price-149, New-392, and a CATH subset show modest F1 improvements over CLEAN at different sequence-identity training cutoffs, with ablations and qualitative attention/active-site visualizations.
strengths: The motivation to inject biological priors (ligand information, hierarchical EC structure, active-site emphasis) into enzyme function prediction is well-grounded and biologically reasonable.

PEEP is evaluated against a relevant set of baselines (CLEAN, ProteInfer, DeepEC, ECPred, BLASTp, Bio-Translator) on the established Price-149 and New-392 benchmarks and shows consistent if modest improvements over CLEAN, especially in precision.
The ablation study (Table 2) separates the contributions of the three proposed components and shows that each yields some incremental gain.
The qualitative analysis correlating attention scores with PocketMiner predictions and with known active-site residues (Fig 4, Fig 5) provides some interpretability support for the attention design.
Authors release code in the supplement and document data filtering (sequence-identity cutoffs), which aids reproducibility.
weaknesses: Reported improvements over CLEAN are small (often 0.2–4.6% F1) and the paper does not report variance across seeds or statistical significance tests. Given that CLEAN's own numbers fluctuate substantially across runs, it is unclear whether the gains are robust. No error bars are provided in any table.

Some headline claims are inconsistent with the tables. The abstract claims 4.6%/3.1%/3.7% F1 improvements on three benchmarks, but Table 1 shows PEEP underperforming CLEAN on recall in several settings, and on New-392 at 30% identity, CLEAN (0.362) is close to or below PEEP (0.374) by only ~1.2 F1 points — not 3.1%. The third benchmark (CATH) numbers in the appendix (F1=0.086 vs 0.049) are on a tiny, idiosyncratically sampled subset (99 sequences from one superfamily) and cannot reasonably support a generalization claim.

The ligand-fusion design has conceptual issues. At inference, the best-performing strategy is to fuse zero vectors (Table A10), meaning ligand information is effectively discarded at test time. This raises the question of whether ligand fusion is acting as a real biological prior or merely as a training-time regularizer/augmentation; the paper does not convincingly resolve this.

The 'generalization to unseen functionalities' claim (Fig 3) is supported only by a UMAP visualization of 6 sequences of a single unseen EC (2.4.1.17). There is no quantitative evaluation of unseen-EC retrieval/clustering, no comparison to CLEAN on the same unseen ECs, and the sample is far too small to support the strong claim made.

The EC-hierarchy-aware loss is intuitive but the function 'diff(y_i, y_j)' is never precisely defined in the main text (its scaling, range, behavior for multi-label EC assignments, etc.), and the ablation (Table 6) shows it only adds ~1.4 F1 points over no-regularization.

Active-site interpretation is weak as evidence: Spearman correlation of 0.21 with PocketMiner (itself a prediction tool, not ground truth) is modest, and the per-residue 'precision 73%' number in A3.2 lacks definition (precision of what, with what threshold?).

Comparison fairness is uncertain: CLEAN was retrained by the authors on their filtered training set with ESM-2 35M and ESM-1b 650M; details of hyperparameter tuning for the baseline (vs PEEP) are sparse, so apples-to-apples comparison cannot be verified.

Presentation issues: the title and parts of the abstract emphasize 'promiscuity', but the paper actually evaluates standard EC number prediction; promiscuity (one enzyme catalyzing multiple reactions) is not directly evaluated. Several tables and the abstract are inconsistent. Many equations/symbols are introduced loosely (e.g., the attention formulation lacks a value matrix, and the self-attention is unusual — it uses q,k from W1,W2 but multiplies P directly by f(x) rather than by a value projection).

Limitations are barely discussed: no discussion of failure cases, of label noise in Swiss-Prot, of overlap between Rhea ligand data and the test EC numbers (which could leak signal), or of the fact that the inference-time best mode discards ligand info.
questions: Can you provide multiple-seed runs with confidence intervals or significance tests for the comparisons in Table 1? The reported deltas are small enough that variance is critical.
Please define precisely the function diff(y_i, y_j) used in the hierarchy-aware loss, including its handling of multi-label EC assignments and whether it is symmetric/normalized.

Why does fusing zero vectors at inference outperform fusing actual ligand embeddings (Table A10)? Does this suggest ligand fusion is acting purely as training-time regularization rather than as a biologically grounded prior at test time?

How was CLEAN retrained on your filtered data? Were its hyperparameters tuned with the same compute budget as PEEP? Can you reproduce CLEAN's originally reported numbers on Price-149/New-392 to validate the baseline implementation?
The paper title and abstract emphasize 'enzyme promiscuity'. Where is promiscuity (multi-function enzymes) explicitly evaluated as a metric, separate from standard multi-label EC prediction?
For the unseen-functionality experiment, can you quantitatively evaluate retrieval/nearest-neighbor metrics on a larger held-out set of unseen ECs and compare against CLEAN on the same set?
Could there be data leakage via the Rhea ligand annotations (which themselves are linked to EC numbers in training)? How is leakage prevented when evaluating on Price/New?

Could you clarify the attention design — there is no explicit value projection W_v; is f(x) used directly as values? Why is sparse top-K attention preferred over standard softmax, beyond the biological intuition?
What is the accuracy/precision metric used for active-site detection in A3.2, exactly? What threshold on attention scores is used to declare a residue 'active'?
reviews.limitations: Limitations are not adequately addressed. The paper does not discuss: variance across seeds, the surprising finding that zero-vector ligand fusion is best at inference, the very small scale of the unseen-functionality experiment, potential data leakage between ligand/Rhea annotations and EC labels, the noisiness of using PocketMiner as a proxy for active sites, and inconsistencies between the title's 'promiscuity' framing and the actual experiments. No ethical or societal-impact discussion is given, though risks are low for this application.
